input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

input.initialPrompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 11 (which would be released in November 2026), System.Text.Json should have a built-in `PascalCase` naming policy. Let me check what's available.

Actually, let me think about this. As of my knowledge cutoff, .NET 9 is the latest released version. .NET 10 would be released in November 2025, and .NET 11 in November 2026. The user is asking about .NET 11 which is in the future.


System.Text.Json currently has `CamelCase` as a built-in policy, and .NET 8 added snake case and kebab case variants, but there's no `PascalCase` policy yet. The default behavior preserves property names as-is, so since C# records generate properties matching the parameter casing, I'd need to check if that's sufficient or if a custom policy is needed. The user mentioned .NET 11, so let me check what's actually installed and work from there. The real question is whether `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` is available—if not, I'll need to be upfront about that limitation. Actually, thinking about this more carefully: System.Text.Json preserves the original property names by default, so if the record parameters are lowercase (`name` and `age`), the JSON output will be lowercase too. To get PascalCase output, I'd either need to rename the record parameters to PascalCase or use a naming policy if one exists.

Let me check what .NET SDK is installed and whether `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` is available in that version.

data.content: .NET 11 preview SDK is available. Let me scaffold the project and check whether `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` is available.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-d261e59cbd044ea4afd2f79fdd0a7220 && mkdir -p PersonApp && cd PersonApp && dotnet new console --framework net11.0 --force -o . 2>&1 | tail -3
